burial.plot_notes: Valhalla Memorial Park Milwaukee per FA

education.notes: Graduated MD 1872. Medical preceptor: uncle Dr. Charles Leuthstrom.

career.notes: Partner with Dr. Leuthstrom, then Dr. William Danforth (1879-1885), then solo. President Wisconsin Homeopathic Society; President Milwaukee Academy of Medicine; 18 years gratuitous service to Milwaukee Protestant Orphan Asylum.

military_service.notes: Enlisted Waukesha Aug 1862; served full 3-year term; honorably discharged Sept 22, 1865. Detailed to special assignments by General Steele. Member GAR, commander E.B. Wolcott Post No.1; staffs of GAR commanders-in-chief Generals Fairchild, Veasy, and Warner.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed from Sweden at ten; shipwrecked; tried again
narrative_blocks.body: Oscar Carlson set out alone from Gothenburg at age ten to join his uncles in America, but his ship was wrecked by a storm on the third day out and drifted helplessly for three months before being towed into Londonderry, Ireland. Undaunted, he took passage again and arrived in New York on January 13, 1854 — months behind schedule and an ocean away from where he had planned to land.

narrative_blocks.header: Held Helena against eighteen thousand Confederates
narrative_blocks.body: At the Battle of Helena, Arkansas on July 4, 1863, Oscar Carlson's regiment — Co. A, 28th Wisconsin Infantry — was part of a Union force of just 4,000 that repulsed a Confederate assault of 18,000 men, one of the Civil War's most lopsided defensive victories. He then served at the Siege of Mobile and in the trenches before Spanish Fort from March 27 to April 8, 1865 — the final weeks of the war in the Western theater — completing a full three-year enlistment before his September 1865 discharge.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother descended from a French aide to Bernadotte
narrative_blocks.body: Oscar Carlson's mother was a Leuthstrom — a descendant of a French family who had followed Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte into Sweden and remained loyal supporters after Bernadotte became King Charles XIV John in 1818. This remarkable ancestry made Wisconsin's prominent homeopathic physician a distant figure in the French imperial entourage that permanently transformed the Swedish royal succession.

sources.label: Memoirs of Milwaukee County (1909), Jerome Anthony Watrous

burial.plot_notes: Valhalla Memorial Park Milwaukee per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of an admiral; her husband held Helena
narrative_blocks.body: Bertha Luceba Strong Carlson was the niece of Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong — the officer who rammed the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee at Mobile Bay — and the wife of Dr. Oscar Wilhelm Carlson, a Stockholm-born physician who had survived a shipwreck at age ten, fought in a 4,000-vs-18,000 battle at Helena, Arkansas, and later became president of the Wisconsin Homeopathic Society. Her family gathered the military heroism and the immigrant drive of mid-nineteenth century America into a single Milwaukee household.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)
Memoirs of Milwaukee County (1909), Jerome Anthony Watrous
